events.text: The patient is a male named Marcus T. Delgado, born in 1965, of Hispanic/Latino ethnicity. He is a former smoker (quit 12 years prior), works as a high school teacher, and has a family history of colorectal cancer in his father. Comorbidities include well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus (on metformin) and mild hypertension at baseline.

At age 58 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition, cT3 N2b M1b) ADENOCARCINOMA OF THE SIGMOID COLON (ICD-O-3 site code C18.7, histology code 8140/3). Synchronous liver metastases were identified in both hepatic lobes (bilobar, 4 lesions, largest 3.8 cm in segment VI). Relevant biomarkers included: MSS (microsatellite stable) by IHC/PCR; KRAS G12D mutation detected; NRAS wild-type; BRAF wild-type (non-V600E); HER2 IHC 1+; TMB low (3 mut/Mb). CEA at diagnosis: 487 ng/mL. CA 19-9: 312 U/mL. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY (colonoscopic biopsy). Primary tumor location: left-sided (sigmoid). Tumor size: 4.5 cm. Lymphovascular invasion: present. Perineural invasion: present. Circumferential resection margin: not applicable at diagnosis. Summary stage: DISTANT.

At age 58 years, the patient had a colonoscopic biopsy pathology result. Specimen: sigmoid colon biopsy. Diagnosis: Moderately differentiated (grade 2) adenocarcinoma of sigmoid colon. Architectural pattern: tubular with focal mucinous features (<10% mucinous component). Immunohistochemistry: MLH1 retained, MSH2 retained, MSH6 retained, PMS2 retained (MMR-proficient / MSS). CDX2 positive. CK20 positive. CK7 negative. SATB2 positive. Ki-67: approximately 65%. No signet ring cells identified.

At age 58 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast (staging CT CAP), which showed cancer. Primary sigmoid colon mass measuring 4.5 x 3.8 cm with pericolonic fat stranding and involvement of 5 regional lymph nodes (largest 1.4 cm). Liver metastases: four lesions identified — segment VI (3.8 cm), segment IVa (2.1 cm), segment II (1.3 cm), and segment VII (0.9 cm). No lung nodules identified. No peritoneal carcinomatosis. No ascites. Bones unremarkable. Impression: Stage IV colorectal cancer with synchronous bilobar liver metastases. No extrahepatic disease other than primary tumor.

At age 58 years, the patient had an MRI of the liver with hepatobiliary contrast (gadoxetate disodium), which showed cancer with metastatic disease. Four hepatic metastases confirmed: segment VI lesion 3.8 cm (arterial enhancement with washout, restricted diffusion), segment IVa lesion 2.1 cm, segment II lesion 1.3 cm, segment VII lesion 0.9 cm. No additional occult lesions identified on hepatobiliary phase. Bilobar distribution precludes upfront surgical resection. Impression: Bilobar colorectal liver metastases, technically unresectable at present; conversion chemotherapy strategy recommended.

At age 58 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for colorectal adenocarcinoma based on a specimen obtained from the sigmoid colon primary tumor (FFPE tissue). Panel: comprehensive solid tumor NGS (523 gene panel). Actionable alterations: KRAS p.G12D (c.35G>A) mutation, variant allele frequency 42%, likely pathogenic driver. Additional somatic mutations: APC p.R1450* nonsense mutation (VAF 48%), TP53 p.R248W missense mutation (VAF 45%), SMAD4 p.R361H missense mutation (VAF 31%), PIK3CA p.E545K mutation (VAF 19%), FBXW7 p.R465H mutation (VAF 14%). Copy number alterations: MYC amplification (6.8-fold), CDK8 gain, chromosome 18q loss (encompassing SMAD4, DCC). Fusions: none detected. Microsatellite instability: MSS (0 unstable loci of 95 examined). TMB: 3.2 mutations/Mb (low). KRAS wild-type codons 12/13 assessment: G12D confirmed; codons 59, 61, 117, 146 wild-type. NRAS exons 2-4: wild-type. BRAF exons 11/15: wild-type. HER2: no amplification (copy number 2.1). Germline findings: no pathogenic germline variants identified in APC, MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, MUTYH. Note: SMAD4 loss and PIK3CA co-mutation may predict reduced benefit from anti-EGFR therapy (irrelevant given RAS mutation), and may confer resistance to some targeted strategies.

At age 58 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board assessment. Discussion involved medical oncology, surgical oncology, interventional radiology, and hepatobiliary surgery. Consensus: primary tumor is not obstructing and does not require immediate resection. Patient is fit for systemic therapy (ECOG PS 1). Given KRAS G12D mutation, anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) is contraindicated. RAS/RAF wild-type panel otherwise, but KRAS G12D precludes anti-EGFR use. Plan: initiate FOLFOX plus bevacizumab with intent of conversion to resectability. Reassess response after 4-6 cycles. Colorectal surgery recommends primary resection can be deferred if no obstruction. Patient counseled on goals of therapy, prognosis, and reproductive/genetic considerations. Cancer present. No response to therapy yet (treatment naive).

At age 58 years, the patient received palliative-intent FOLFOX (oxaliplatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, then 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV day 1, given every 2 weeks. Cycle 1 of planned 12 cycles. Pre-medications: ondansetron, dexamethasone, lorazepam. Hydration protocol initiated. Neuropathy baseline assessment documented (0/10). Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg at treatment initiation.

At age 58 years (approximately 6 weeks into FOLFOX/bevacizumab, after cycle 3), the patient developed grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy in the fingertips and toes bilaterally — described as intermittent tingling and numbness, worse with cold exposure (cold-induced dysesthesias, classic oxaliplatin-related acute neuropathy). No functional impairment. Management: patient educated on cold avoidance, gloves in cold weather. No dose reduction required at this time. Additionally, blood pressure noted to be elevated at 154/96 mmHg, new grade 1 hypertension attributed to bevacizumab. Amlodipine 5 mg daily initiated in coordination with primary care physician.

At age 58 years (after 4 cycles of FOLFOX/bevacizumab, approximately 8 weeks from treatment start), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with partial response. Liver metastases: segment VI lesion decreased from 3.8 to 2.9 cm (24% decrease), segment IVa from 2.1 to 1.6 cm (24% decrease), segment II from 1.3 to 1.0 cm (23% decrease), segment VII from 0.9 to 0.6 cm (33% decrease). Primary sigmoid tumor appears slightly smaller (3.9 cm). No new lesions. No lung nodules. RECIST 1.1: sum of target lesions decreased from 8.1 cm to 6.1 cm, representing 25% decrease — PARTIAL RESPONSE. CEA trending down from 487 to 198 ng/mL. Impression: Partial response to FOLFOX/bevacizumab. Continue therapy; re-evaluate for potential conversion resection.

At age 58 years (cycle 4 response assessment), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing cancer present with response to therapy. CEA 198 ng/mL, improving. Patient tolerating therapy reasonably well. Peripheral neuropathy now grade 1-2, worse in fingertips — continued cold dysesthesias plus emerging persistent numbness in feet. Hypertension now controlled on amlodipine (BP 142/88 mmHg). Discussed with hepatobiliary surgery: bilobar disease still present, insufficient hepatic reserve for resection after 4 cycles. Plan: continue FOLFOX/bevacizumab to 12 cycles, reassess liver resectability. ECOG PS 1.

At age 58 years (approximately cycle 8, 4 months into treatment), the patient developed worsening peripheral sensory neuropathy now grade 2: persistent numbness and tingling in bilateral feet and fingertips, affecting fine motor tasks (difficulty buttoning shirt, writing), though still ambulatory without assistance. Oxaliplatin dose reduced to 75 mg/m2 (from 85 mg/m2) per standard dose modification guidelines. Hypertension progressed to grade 2 (BP 162/100 mmHg despite amlodipine 5 mg); amlodipine dose increased to 10 mg daily. Patient referred to cardiology for blood pressure management.

At age 58 years (cycle 10), the patient developed grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy: numbness now involving bilateral feet to mid-calf and bilateral hands to the wrists; patient has difficulty walking on uneven surfaces and has dropped objects on 3 occasions. Oxaliplatin discontinued per institutional protocol (cumulative dose approximately 760 mg/m2). Patient transitioned to modified FOLFOX without oxaliplatin (5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab) to complete the planned treatment course. Neuropathy graded as grade 3 per CTCAE v5.0. Neurology consultation obtained; electromyography/nerve conduction studies ordered. Gabapentin 300 mg TID initiated for neuropathic pain management.

At age 58 years (after 12 cycles of planned FOLFOX/bevacizumab, with cycles 11-12 as 5-FU/LV/bevacizumab due to oxaliplatin discontinuation, approximately 6 months from treatment start), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with partial response (best response). Liver metastases: segment VI lesion 2.3 cm (from 3.8 cm, 39% decrease), segment IVa 1.2 cm (43% decrease), segment II 0.7 cm (46% decrease), segment VII 0.4 cm (56% decrease). Primary sigmoid tumor: 3.2 cm (29% decrease from baseline). No new lesions. No lung nodules identified. RECIST 1.1: sum of target lesions decreased from 8.1 cm to 4.6 cm, representing 43% decrease — PARTIAL RESPONSE (confirmed). CEA: 87 ng/mL (down from 487). CA 19-9: 64 U/mL. Impression: Ongoing partial response. Liver metastases reduced but hepatobiliary surgery notes continued bilobar distribution; surgical resection still not feasible given need for adequate future liver remnant.

At age 58 years, the patient had an EMG/nerve conduction study (NCS) performed by neurology. Findings: Sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy, predominantly sensory, axonal pattern affecting bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sural sensory nerve action potential (SNAP) reduced bilaterally. Peroneal and tibial motor nerve conduction velocities mildly reduced. No evidence of demyelinating features. Conclusion: Findings consistent with oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy, length-dependent, axonal sensorimotor pattern. Prognosis: partial recovery expected over months to years after oxaliplatin cessation; complete resolution uncertain given grade 3 severity.

At age 58 years (approximately 6 months from initial treatment start, following completion of 12 cycles of induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab), the patient transitioned to maintenance therapy with 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours plus leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV plus bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (maintenance 5-FU/LV/bevacizumab). Rationale: standard maintenance approach per OPTIMOX1 strategy and CAIRO3 data, intended to maintain disease control while reducing cumulative oxaliplatin toxicity (already discontinued). Peripheral neuropathy grade 3 at transition, planned monitoring for improvement.

At age 58 years (2 months into maintenance therapy, approximately 8 months from diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing cancer present, disease stable (no progression on maintenance). CEA 79 ng/mL (stable). Patient tolerating maintenance 5-FU/bevacizumab well. Peripheral neuropathy now improving to grade 2 — still present in feet and hands but less severe, gabapentin providing partial relief. Hypertension now well controlled on amlodipine 10 mg plus lisinopril 10 mg (BP 134/84 mmHg). Patient returned to part-time teaching duties. No new symptoms. ECOG PS 1. Plan: continue maintenance, next CT restaging in 6 weeks.

At age 58 years (approximately 10 months from initial diagnosis, 4 months into maintenance therapy), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with progression. New findings: Two new pulmonary nodules — right lower lobe 1.1 cm and right upper lobe 0.7 cm — new from prior scan, highly suspicious for metastases. Liver metastases: slight increase in segment VI lesion (2.5 cm vs. 2.3 cm), segment IVa stable (1.2 cm), segment II stable (0.7 cm), segment VII 0.5 cm (was 0.4 cm). Primary sigmoid colon mass: 3.3 cm (stable). RECIST 1.1: sum of target lesions increased from 4.6 cm to 6.1 cm (new lung lesions added to target sum), representing 33% increase — PROGRESSIVE DISEASE by RECIST (new lesions). CEA: 142 ng/mL (rising from 79). CA 19-9: 108 U/mL (rising). Impression: Radiographic disease progression on maintenance therapy with new pulmonary metastatic disease. Clinical decision: Discontinue maintenance, initiate second-line therapy.

At age 58 years (at time of progression, 10 months from diagnosis), the patient had a CT-guided biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule. Specimen: lung core needle biopsy. Diagnosis: Metastatic adenocarcinoma, consistent with colorectal primary. Morphology: moderately differentiated glandular architecture with dirty necrosis. Immunohistochemistry: CK20 positive, CDX2 positive, CK7 negative, TTF-1 negative, SATB2 positive. Impression: Morphology and immunoprofile consistent with colorectal primary metastasis to lung. KRAS G12D confirmed by targeted PCR on lung biopsy tissue (concordant with primary).

At age 58 years (10 months from diagnosis), the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board re-evaluation following documented disease progression. New pulmonary metastases confirmed as colorectal origin. Disease now stage IV with both hepatic and pulmonary metastases. ECOG PS 1. Discussion: patient progressed through oxaliplatin-based regimen (and oxaliplatin was discontinued for toxicity). Second-line options reviewed: FOLFIRI with either bevacizumab continuation (per TML/ML18147 data) or ramucirumab (per RAISE trial data). Given bevacizumab was used in first-line, options include ramucirumab (VEGFR-2 targeted antibody). KRAS G12D status precludes anti-EGFR therapy. No approved KRAS G12D-specific targeted therapy available outside clinical trial (note: MRTX1133 not yet approved). Enrollment in clinical trial discussed but patient declined. Decision: FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab per RAISE trial regimen. Cancer present, progression documented.

At age 58-59 years (month 10 from diagnosis), the patient received second-line FOLFIRI (irinotecan 180 mg/m2 IV over 90 minutes day 1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1, 5-fluorouracil 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1, then 5-fluorouracil 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours) plus ramucirumab 8 mg/kg IV day 1, every 2 weeks. Cycle 1 initiated. Pre-medications: ondansetron, dexamethasone, atropine (prophylactic). Baseline ECOG PS 1. UGT1A1 genotyping performed prior to irinotecan: patient is UGT1A1 *1/*28 (heterozygous), indicating intermediate irinotecan metabolizer — standard starting dose appropriate, monitor for toxicity.

At age 59 years (approximately cycle 3 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, 6 weeks into second-line therapy), the patient developed grade 2 diarrhea — up to 6-7 loose stools per day, occurring in delayed fashion (days 2-5 post-infusion), consistent with irinotecan-related delayed diarrhea. Management: loperamide 4 mg at first loose stool, then 2 mg every 2 hours until 12 hours diarrhea-free per standard protocol. Irinotecan dose reduced to 150 mg/m2 (from 180 mg/m2) for subsequent cycles. Patient educated on hydration. Residual peripheral neuropathy continued to improve (now grade 1), gabapentin tapered.

At age 59 years (cycle 5 of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab), the patient developed worsening hypertension grade 3 with blood pressure measurements of 174/108 mmHg on two separate readings, attributed to ramucirumab (anti-VEGFR-2 mechanism). Ramucirumab held for 1 cycle. Antihypertensive regimen intensified: lisinopril increased from 10 to 20 mg daily, amlodipine continued at 10 mg, added hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg daily. Cardiology co-management continued. Blood pressure responded to regimen intensification (142/88 mmHg). Ramucirumab resumed at full dose with close BP monitoring each cycle.

At age 59 years (after 4 cycles of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, approximately 3 months into second-line therapy, 13 months from diagnosis), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with mixed response/stable disease. Lung nodules: right lower lobe lesion stable at 1.1 cm, right upper lobe lesion stable at 0.7 cm (no new pulmonary nodules). Liver metastases: segment VI lesion 2.3 cm (down from 2.5 cm), segment IVa 1.1 cm (stable), segment II 0.7 cm (stable), segment VII 0.4 cm (stable). Primary sigmoid colon mass: 3.1 cm (stable). RECIST 1.1: sum of target lesions 5.7 cm (from 6.1 cm), representing 7% decrease — STABLE DISEASE (no PR threshold reached, no PD). CEA: 112 ng/mL (slight decrease from 142). CA 19-9: 94 U/mL. Impression: Disease stabilization on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. Continue current regimen.

At age 59 years (approximately 14 months from diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment showing cancer present, with disease stable on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. CEA 112 ng/mL (stable). Patient is managing reasonably well on second-line therapy. Neuropathy continues to improve (grade 1 now, intermittent tingling in feet only). Hypertension managed on triple antihypertensive regimen, BP controlled. Irinotecan-related diarrhea manageable with dose reduction and loperamide. Patient remains ECOG PS 1. Discussed prognosis and goals of care — patient wishes to continue active treatment, focused on quality of life and time with family. Palliative care consultation offered; patient agrees to outpatient palliative care involvement for symptom management co-management. Plan: continue FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, repeat imaging in 8 weeks.

At age 59 years (approximately 17 months from diagnosis, 7 months into second-line therapy), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with progressive disease. New findings: Three additional pulmonary nodules — left lower lobe 0.8 cm, right middle lobe 0.6 cm, left upper lobe (lingula) 0.5 cm. Existing lung nodules stable. Liver metastases: segment VI lesion increased to 2.9 cm (from 2.3 cm), new segment V lesion 0.8 cm. Segment IVa, II, VII stable. Primary sigmoid tumor: 3.4 cm (slight increase). RECIST 1.1: sum of target lesions increased from 5.7 cm to 8.5 cm (49% increase, including new lesions) — PROGRESSIVE DISEASE. CEA: 287 ng/mL (markedly rising). CA 19-9: 201 U/mL. Impression: Disease progression on FOLFIRI/ramucizumab with new pulmonary and hepatic lesions. Transition to third-line therapy indicated.

At age 59 years (17 months from diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment demonstrating disease progression on second-line FOLFIRI/ramucirumab. ECOG PS 2 (some fatigue and decreased activity, now spending >50% of waking hours ambulatory but with limitations). Discussed third-line options: regorafenib (standard third-line, CORRECT trial), TAS-102/trifluridine-tipiracil (RECOURSE trial), or combination TAS-102 plus bevacizumab (SUNLIGHT trial data). Clinical trial options re-discussed including KRAS G12D targeted therapies in development. Patient counseled on declining performance status and need to balance efficacy against quality of life. Patient and family express clear desire to continue treatment if tolerable. Plan: initiate TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil) plus bevacizumab per SUNLIGHT regimen as third-line therapy. Cancer present, progression documented. Palliative care team actively involved.

At age 59 years (month 17 from diagnosis), the patient received third-line TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) 35 mg/m2 orally twice daily with food on days 1-5 and 8-12 of each 28-day cycle, plus bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (days 1 and 15 of each cycle), per the SUNLIGHT trial regimen. Cycle 1 initiated. Patient instructed on oral medication compliance. Antiemetic prophylaxis: ondansetron PRN. Complete blood count monitoring every 2 weeks given myelosuppression risk.

At age 59 years (cycle 2 of TAS-102/bevacizumab), the patient developed grade 3 neutropenia (absolute neutrophil count 0.7 x10^9/L) on day 15 CBC check. No fever; no infectious symptoms. Management: TAS-102 dose held on days 8-12 of cycle 2. G-CSF (filgrastim) administered for 5 days. ANC recovered to 1.9 x10^9/L by day 22. TAS-102 dose reduced to 30 mg/m2 for cycle 3 per dose modification guidelines. No hospitalization required. Bevacizumab held pending ANC recovery.

At age 59 years (approximately 20 months from diagnosis, after 3 cycles of TAS-102/bevacizumab), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with stable disease. Lung nodules: multiple bilateral nodules as previously documented, none significantly increased. Right lower lobe largest nodule 1.2 cm (minimally increased from 1.1 cm). Liver metastases: segment VI lesion 3.1 cm (slight increase from 2.9 cm); new segment V lesion 0.9 cm (from 0.8 cm). Sum of target lesions: 9.1 cm (from 8.5 cm at progression), representing 7% increase — borderline stable vs. minor progression (below 20% threshold for definitive PD by RECIST). CEA: 312 ng/mL (rising). CA 19-9: 245 U/mL. Impression: Disease marginally stable on TAS-102/bevacizumab; minimal interval change. Continue therapy; close monitoring. Disease trajectory concerning.

At age 59 years (approximately 21 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist office assessment with palliative care co-management. Cancer present. Performance status declined to ECOG PS 2-3 — significant fatigue, decreased appetite, weight loss of 8 lbs over past 2 months, increased abdominal discomfort. Patient and family (wife and two adult children) present for goals-of-care discussion. Explored patient's values and wishes: patient desires to avoid hospitalization if possible, prioritizes time at home with family, agrees that quality of life is increasingly important relative to quantity of time. Decision: continue TAS-102/bevacizumab at reduced dose if tolerated; increase palliative care involvement; initiate formal advance care planning including POLST/MOLST completion. Discussed hospice as future option when no longer benefiting from treatment. Referred to clinical dietitian for nutritional support. Symptom management: prochlorperazine for nausea, methylphenidate 5 mg AM for fatigue, acetaminophen for abdominal discomfort. Cancer present, slow progression.

At age 59 years (approximately 23 months from diagnosis, after 5 cycles of TAS-102/bevacizumab), the patient had a CT CAP restaging scan, which showed cancer with progressive disease. Significant interval increase: liver metastases now extensive with segment VI lesion 4.2 cm, new lesions in segments III and VIII (1.4 cm and 1.1 cm respectively), total hepatic tumor burden increased substantially. Lung nodules: bilateral, now 6 nodules identified, multiple >1 cm. New small volume ascites. No brain metastases assessed. RECIST 1.1: progressive disease. CEA: 587 ng/mL (markedly elevated). CA 19-9: 412 U/mL. Impression: Significant multiorgan disease progression on third-line therapy. Patient now has hepatic, pulmonary, and peritoneal involvement.

At age 59 years (23 months from initial diagnosis), the patient had an oncologist and palliative care team joint assessment. Cancer present with progression through three lines of therapy. ECOG PS 3. Patient now with worsening fatigue, anorexia, early satiety, mild abdominal distension from ascites. Patient and family meeting conducted. After thorough discussion of disease trajectory, expected prognosis (weeks to months), goals of care, patient elected to transition to comfort-focused hospice care. All cytotoxic and targeted therapies discontinued. Enrolled in home hospice program. Symptom management plan: opioids for pain/dyspnea (low-dose morphine IR), lorazepam PRN for anxiety, haloperidol PRN for nausea, and continued dexamethasone 2 mg daily for appetite/energy. Goals: maintain comfort, maximize time at home with family, avoid invasive procedures. Primary oncologist to remain available for consultation. Last clinic visit with oncology team at this encounter.

documents.text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY — OUTPATIENT CLINIC NOTE
Response Assessment Visit — Cycle 4 FOLFOX/Avastin

---

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Medical Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Gastrointestinal Oncology
VISIT TYPE: Established Patient — Treatment Response Assessment

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Marcus T. Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic/Latino male with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D mutated, MSS) with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases who presents today for cycle 4 response assessment following CT restaging showing partial response to FOLFOX plus Avastin.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Delgado is well-known to our gastrointestinal oncology service. He was initially diagnosed with stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 8-9 weeks ago following presentation with a change in bowel habits, left-sided abdominal cramping, and an elevated CEA of 487 ng/mL. Colonoscopy demonstrated a 4.5 cm sigmoid mass, biopsy-confirmed as moderately differentiated (grade 2) adenocarcinoma, CDX2+, CK20+, CK7-, SATB2+, MMR-proficient (MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2 all retained by IHC). Staging CT and MRI liver confirmed four bilobar hepatic metastases (largest 3.8 cm in segment VI), with no evidence of extrahepatic disease beyond the primary and liver at that time.

Comprehensive molecular profiling via NGS (523-gene panel) from the sigmoid primary revealed KRAS p.G12D mutation (VAF 42%, confirmed pathogenic driver), with co-occurring alterations in APC p.R1450* (VAF 48%), TP53 p.R248W (VAF 45%), SMAD4 p.R361H (VAF 31%), PIK3CA p.E545K (VAF 19%), and FBXW7 p.R465H (VAF 14%). MYC amplification and 18q loss also identified. The KRAS G12D mutation confirmed ineligibility for anti-EGFR therapy (Erbitux or pani), and MSS/low-TMB status (3.2 mut/Mb) confirmed ineligibility for pembro or other checkpoint inhibitors in this frontline setting.

Following multidisciplinary tumor board discussion — with consensus from medical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, and interventional radiology — the patient was deemed unresectable at presentation given bilobar hepatic distribution and the need to preserve adequate future liver remnant. A conversion chemotherapy strategy was elected, with the primary sigmoid tumor assessed as non-obstructing and not requiring urgent surgical intervention. Treatment was initiated with FOLFOX (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 D1, leucovorin 400 mg/m2 D1, 5-FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus D1, then 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 CI over 46 hours) plus bev 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks.

The patient has now completed 4 cycles of FOLFOX/Avastin. Restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed after completion of cycle 4 (approximately 8 weeks from treatment initiation) and demonstrated encouraging tumor reduction: the segment VI hepatic lesion decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.9 cm, segment IVa from 2.1 to 1.6 cm, segment II from 1.3 to 1.0 cm, and segment VII from 0.9 to 0.6 cm. The primary sigmoid mass appears slightly smaller at approximately 3.9 cm. No new lesions were identified, and no pulmonary metastases were seen. CEA has trended down from a baseline of 487 ng/mL to 198 ng/mL — a 59% reduction — which is gratifying and consistent with the radiographic response.

This was reviewed informally with our hepatobiliary surgical colleague ahead of today's clinic visit. Despite the objective tumor shrinkage, the bilobar distribution of hepatic disease remains unchanged (lesions persist in segments II, IVa, VI, and VII), and the estimated future liver remnant following any hypothetical resection of all disease-bearing segments would be insufficient — estimated at less than 25-30% of total liver volume — making conversion hepatectomy not yet feasible. The consensus is to continue toward the planned 12-cycle course and reassess at that endpoint.

The patient presents today for formal review of imaging findings, discussion of toxicity management, and determination of the treatment plan going forward.

From a symptom standpoint, Mr. Delgado reports the following:

- NEUROPATHY: He has been experiencing tingling and numbness in his fingertips and toes since approximately cycle 3, initially described as intermittent and cold-triggered. At today's visit, he notes the neuropathy has become somewhat more persistent, particularly in the fingertips, where he experiences near-constant tingling. The classic cold-induced dysesthesias remain prominent — he describes experiencing sharp, shooting sensations when handling cold objects and has been consistently using gloves since we counseled him on cold avoidance. He is also noticing early, emerging sensory change in the dorsum of his feet, though this is less prominent than the hand symptoms. No significant functional impairment reported at this time — he can still button his shirt and write on the board at school, though he notes some slowing. He rates the neuropathy as 4/10 in bothersome-ness and 2/10 for pain.

- HYPERTENSION: He was placed on amlodipine 5 mg daily at approximately cycle 3 for new Avastin-related hypertension (grade 1, BP 154/96 mmHg). Today's blood pressure is 142/88 mmHg, which represents meaningful improvement though is still mildly above goal. He denies headaches, visual changes, chest pain, or shortness of breath. He has been coordinating with his primary care physician regarding antihypertensive management and reports medication compliance.

- GI SYMPTOMS: He reports mild intermittent nausea on the day of infusion and the following morning, well-managed with the prescribed prochlorperazine. Appetite remains intact. No significant mucositis. No constipation or diarrhea beyond what is expected. Bowel habits are generally every 1-2 days, semi-formed stool, no blood noted.

- FATIGUE: He describes mild to moderate fatigue, particularly during the week following infusion (days 3-7), with recovery toward his baseline by the second week. He has returned to part-time teaching duties on a modified schedule, which he finds meaningful and wishes to continue as long as possible.

- PORT/INFUSION SITE: No issues with the implanted port. No signs of infection or thrombosis.

He denies chest pain, dyspnea, hematochezia, melena, significant abdominal pain, skin rash, or urinary symptoms. He has not experienced any grade 3-4 toxicities to date requiring hospitalization or emergency evaluation.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

- Constitutional: Mild fatigue (as above). No fever, chills, or sweats. Weight stable (patient reports no appreciable weight change, confirmed approximately flat on clinic scale).
- HEENT: No headaches, no visual disturbances.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or hemoptysis.
- Cardiovascular: Mild hypertension as noted; no chest pain, palpitations, edema.
- Gastrointestinal: Mild nausea controlled with antiemetics. No significant mucositis, diarrhea, constipation, rectal bleeding, or melena. No abdominal pain beyond mild baseline discomfort.
- Neurological: Peripheral sensory neuropathy grade 1-2 as described — fingertips bilaterally (predominant), toes and dorsal feet (emerging); cold-induced dysesthesias ongoing. No gait instability, no weakness, no fall.
- Musculoskeletal: No bone pain or new joint complaints.
- Dermatologic: No hand-foot syndrome, no rash.
- Genitourinary: No hematuria, no dysuria. Proteinuria not formally checked since last visit — will obtain today's urine spot protein/creatinine ratio given Avastin use.
- Hematologic: No unusual bleeding or bruising.
- Endocrine/Metabolic: Diabetes well managed on metformin, home blood glucose monitoring readings acceptable per patient.
- Psychiatric: Mild anxiety about diagnosis and prognosis, which he describes as manageable. Good family and social support.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS) — as above
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well controlled on metformin, HbA1c 6.8% at diagnosis
3. Hypertension — new/worsening in context of Avastin; now on amlodipine
4. Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (emerging, grade 1-2)
5. Former smoker (quit approximately 12 years prior, approximately 15-20 pack-year history)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
Mr. Delgado is a high school teacher (science), lives with his wife, and has two adult children. He is a former smoker who quit approximately 12 years ago. ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. STAGE IV SIGMOID COLON ADENOCARCINOMA (KRAS G12D, MSS), WITH BILOBAR HEPATIC METASTASES — PARTIAL RESPONSE TO FOLFOX/bev:

Mr. Delgado is showing a meaningful, though not yet sufficient-for-resection, partial response to first-line FOLFOX plus bev after 4 cycles. The CEA trajectory is encouraging, having dropped from 487 to 198 ng/mL — a 59% reduction — which mirrors the radiographic findings. The goal of the conversion chemotherapy strategy remains operative: to reduce hepatic tumor burden to a point where complete oncologic resection with adequate FLR is feasible.

Given the continued bilobar involvement and insufficient FLR, the plan is to continue with the full intended 12-cycle course of FOLFOX/Avastin. Formal re-presentation to the hepatobiliary multidisciplinary team will occur after cycle 8 for interim reassessment and again after cycle 12 for definitive surgical planning. We will maintain FOLFOX at current doses (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2) unless dose-limiting toxicity mandates reduction.

Molecular landscape: KRAS G12D mutation — anti-EGFR therapies remain contraindicated. BRAF wild-type. MSS. PIK3CA p.E545K co-mutation and SMAD4 loss are noted but do not change current management (anti-EGFR is already precluded by KRAS mutation). No clinical trials with KRAS G12D-directed agents are currently available for this patient; this will continue to be monitored.

2. Eloxatin-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY, GRADE 1-2:

Mr. Delgado has developed grade 1-2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, with predominantly cold dysesthesias and emerging persistent numbness in the fingertips and early sensory changes in the feet. This is an expected, cumulative, and unfortunately dose-limiting toxicity of oxali. At this point, the neuropathy is grade 1-2 and is not yet meeting criteria for dose modification (our institutional threshold for Eloxatin dose reduction is grade 2 with functional impairment or grade 3).

Management plan:
- Continue cold avoidance counseling: cold beverages, cold air exposure, cold tap water — gloves, warm socks, avoidance of cold foods/drinks during infusion days and the following 5-7 days.
- Maintain intravenous calcium and magnesium infusions with each cycle (though evidence for neuropathy prevention is limited, this is our institutional standard practice and is benign).
- Patient to notify our team immediately if neuropathy progresses to grade 2 with functional impairment (difficulty with ADLs) or grade 3 at any time — at which point oxali dose reduction to 75 mg/m2 or potential discontinuation will be implemented.
- Baseline neurological exam documented today; will reassess at each visit.
- No pharmacologic neuropathy prophylaxis agents (duloxetine has modest neuropathy treatment data; vitamin B6, vitamin E, and glutamine not recommended based on current evidence).
- Plan to reassess at next visit (cycle 6 or post-cycle 6 check).

3. bev-RELATED HYPERTENSION, GRADE 1 (IMPROVING):

Blood pressure today is 142/88 mmHg on amlodipine 5 mg daily, which is improved from the peak of 154/96 mmHg at cycle 3 when antihypertensive therapy was initiated. This remains mildly above target (goal BP <140/90 mmHg in cancer patients; ideally <130/80 mmHg). Grade 1 bev-related hypertension managed appropriately with calcium channel blockade. No proteinuria, no thromboembolic symptoms to suggest other bev toxicities.

Plan:
- Continue amlodipine 5 mg daily. If BP at next visit remains above 140/90, will coordinate with PCP to uptitrate amlodipine to 10 mg or add an ACE inhibitor/ARB.
- Urine spot protein/creatinine ratio obtained today: 0.18 — no significant proteinuria, Avastin safe to continue.
- Avastin to continue at 5 mg/kg. Threshold for holding: diastolic >110 mmHg or any grade 3 hypertension uncontrolled on oral agents.
- Patient reminded of symptoms warranting emergency evaluation: severe headache, visual changes, hypertensive urgency/emergency symptoms.

Continue metformin 1000 mg BID. HbA1c 6.9% — acceptable. Monitoring ongoing. No adjustments required. Patient reminded that dexamethasone premedication can transiently elevate blood glucose on infusion days — home monitoring encouraged and patient is aware.

5. CANCER-RELATED FATIGUE:
Mild to moderate, expected in the context of active chemotherapy with FOLFOX. Pattern consistent with expected nadir (days 3-7 post-infusion). ECOG PS 1 maintained. Patient has returned to part-time teaching, which is a positive functional indicator. No specific pharmacologic intervention at this time. Encouraged adequate rest, light activity as tolerated, nutrition. Patient declines formal referral to integrative oncology/exercise rehabilitation at this time but will reconsider.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

F-Waves: As noted above — mildly prolonged in bilateral lower extremities (peroneal, tibial); upper extremity F-waves (median, ulnar) within normal limits.

H-Reflex (right soleus recording, tibial nerve stimulation at popliteal fossa):
  H-reflex absent bilaterally (right and left).
  Interpretation: Consistent with distal sensory axon dysfunction affecting the reflex arc; alternative consideration of diabetes-related contribution cannot be fully excluded.

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
DIAGNOSTIC SUMMARY AND INTERPRETATION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

The electrodiagnostic findings in Mr. Delgado reveal a SENSORIMOTOR PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY with the following characteristics:

1. DISTRIBUTION: Symmetric, length-dependent (stocking-glove distribution), affecting bilateral lower extremities more severely than upper extremities. Consistent with a "dying-back" axonopathy.

2. PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: PREDOMINANTLY AXONAL pattern — characterized by reduced SNAP and CMAP amplitudes with relative preservation of conduction velocities. Mild conduction velocity slowing in lower extremities is proportional to loss of large myelinated fiber density (secondary demyelination from axon loss), rather than primary demyelination. No evidence of segmental demyelination, conduction block, or temporal dispersion.

3. SENSORY vs. MOTOR: Predominantly sensory, with modest motor involvement limited to distal lower extremity muscles. Upper extremity motor studies normal.

4. ACTIVE vs. CHRONIC: Active denervation (fibrillation potentials) in distal lower extremity and intrinsic hand muscles indicates ongoing axonal injury or incomplete recovery. Chronic neurogenic changes (polyphasic, increased-duration MUAPs) indicate subacute-to-chronic course.

5. ABSENT H-REFLEXES: Indicates significant large-fiber sensory dysfunction in the distal lower extremities.

6. DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS CONSIDERATION: The electrodiagnostic pattern is most consistent with oxaliplatin-induced peripheral neuropathy (cumulative dose approximately 760 mg/m2), which characteristically produces a predominantly sensory, length-dependent axonal neuropathy targeting dorsal root ganglion (DRG) neurons. Although the patient has a known diagnosis of type 2 diabetes mellitus, the severity and distribution of electrodiagnostic abnormalities exceed what would be expected for mild diabetic polyneuropathy alone, and the clinical timeline correlates directly with oxaliplatin exposure. The contribution of diabetic polyneuropathy to the overall picture cannot be excluded and may have lowered the threshold for oxaliplatin neuropathy at this cumulative dose. No features of hereditary neuropathy (no family history; no high-arched feet; no demyelinating pattern).

─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
CONCLUSION
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

ELECTRODIAGNOSTIC CONCLUSION: SENSORIMOTOR PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY, PREDOMINANTLY SENSORY, LENGTH-DEPENDENT, AXONAL PATTERN, AFFECTING BILATERAL UPPER AND LOWER EXTREMITIES.

The findings are consistent with OXALIPLATIN-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY in a patient with known exposure to cumulative high-dose oxaliplatin (approximately 760 mg/m2) for colorectal cancer treatment. The pattern reflects DRG neuronopathy/axonopathy characteristic of platinum compound neurotoxicity.

SEVERITY CLASSIFICATION: Moderate-to-severe electrodiagnostic changes in lower extremities; mild changes in upper extremities.

PROGNOSTIC NOTE: Based on existing literature regarding oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy of this severity (corresponding to clinical grade 3 per CTCAE v5.0), partial recovery is anticipated following cessation of oxaliplatin. However, complete resolution is uncertain, particularly given the severity at time of oxaliplatin discontinuation. Recovery, when it occurs, typically follows a slow time course of months to years, with sensory symptoms generally improving more than motor deficits. The presence of mild diabetic neuropathy as a potential contributing comorbidity may affect the extent of recovery.

ANCILLARY STUDIES:
  - Serum B12, folate, TSH, SPEP with immunofixation (to exclude other treatable causes): Results pending at time of this report.
  - Genetic testing for hereditary neuropathy (e.g., PMP22 duplication/deletion): Not indicated given clinical picture; deferred unless recovery is unexpectedly poor.

GROSS DESCRIPTION:

Specimen A is received in formalin, labeled with the patient's name and medical record number, and designated "right lower lobe lung biopsy." The container holds three cylindrical core needle biopsy tissue fragments. The cores range from 1.2 to 1.5 cm in length and approximately 0.1 cm in greatest diameter. MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION:

Histologic sections of the core needle biopsy reveal fragments of lung parenchyma with near-complete effacement by a moderately differentiated infiltrating adenocarcinoma. The neoplastic process is characterized by irregular, angulated glandular structures and tubular formations embedded within a desmoplastic stromal background. The glands vary in size and shape, with some exhibiting back-to-back crowding and focal cribriform architecture. A subset of glands demonstrates central luminal "dirty necrosis" — the hallmark intraluminal granular eosinophilic necrotic debris that is characteristically associated with colorectal adenocarcinoma — a feature readily identified on multiple sections. No lepidic, acinar (primary pulmonary), papillary, or micropapillary architectural patterns typically associated with primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma are identified.

The tumor cells are columnar to cuboidal in shape, with elongated pseudostratified hyperchromatic nuclei displaying coarse chromatin, prominent nucleoli, and variable mitotic activity (approximately 6–8 mitoses per 10 high-power fields). Nuclear pleomorphism is moderate. The cytoplasm is pale eosinophilic to amphophilic; no significant intracytoplasmic mucin accumulation is identified to suggest a mucinous or signet ring cell variant. Rare scattered tumor cells with slightly more open, vesicular nuclei are present, but signet ring cells are not identified.

The adjacent lung parenchyma shows focal entrapped alveolar architecture at the periphery of the biopsy cores, with mild reactive pneumocyte hyperplasia. No features of primary pulmonary malignancy, granulomatous inflammation, or organizing pneumonia are identified in the residual lung parenchyma.

Lymphovascular invasion is suspected focally based on neoplastic glands adjacent to thin-walled vascular structures, though definitive assessment is limited on core biopsy material.

DIAGNOSIS:

A. RIGHT LOWER LOBE LUNG, CT-GUIDED CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY:
   — METASTATIC ADENOCARCINOMA, CONSISTENT WITH COLORECTAL PRIMARY
   — Morphologic features including glandular architecture with intraluminal dirty necrosis and absence of lepidic or primary pulmonary adenocarcinoma patterns are characteristic of metastatic colorectal origin
   — See ancillary studies below

COMMENT:

The morphologic and immunohistochemical findings in this right lower lobe lung core needle biopsy are diagnostic of metastatic adenocarcinoma with an origin from the colorectum. The combination of glandular architecture with intraluminal dirty necrosis, strong and diffuse CK20, CDX2, and SATB2 positivity, with negativity for CK7, TTF-1, and Napsin A, effectively establishes this as a colorectal metastasis rather than a primary pulmonary neoplasm. Furthermore, the detection of the identical KRAS p.G12D driver mutation (VAF 38%) in this biopsy specimen is concordant with the patient's known primary sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D VAF 42% on primary tumor NGS), providing additional molecular confirmation of metastatic origin.

These findings, taken in the context of the clinical history of Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with known hepatic metastases and interval development of bilateral pulmonary nodules on surveillance imaging, support the radiographic impression of pulmonary metastatic disease from colorectal carcinoma.

MMR IHC results will be reported as an addendum upon completion.

Correlation with clinical, radiographic, and prior molecular findings is recommended.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Delgado was originally diagnosed with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma approximately 10 months ago. At that time, staging CT and liver MRI revealed a 4.5 cm primary sigmoid tumor with regional nodal involvement (cT3 N2b) and synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases in four hepatic segments (largest 3.8 cm in segment VI). The tumor was confirmed to be KRAS G12D-mutant (VAF 42%), NRAS wild-type, BRAF wild-type, MSS with low TMB (3.2 mut/Mb), ruling out anti-EGFR therapy and immune checkpoint inhibitor candidacy at the outset. His initial NGS panel also identified co-mutations in APC, TP53, SMAD4, PIK3CA, and FBXW7 — a molecular landscape consistent with a relatively aggressive CRC phenotype.

He was enrolled in first-line treatment with FOLFOX (Eloxatin 85 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU bolus/infusional) plus bev 5 mg/kg every 2 weeks. He completed a total of 12 cycles over approximately 6 months. Radiographically, he achieved a meaningful partial response: his hepatic metastases decreased significantly (e.g., segment VI lesion from 3.8 to 2.3 cm), and CEA fell from 487 ng/mL at baseline to 87 ng/mL at the end of induction. Unfortunately, he developed progressive Eloxatin-induced peripheral neuropathy during induction therapy. By cycle 10, neuropathy had progressed to grade 3 — bilateral distal sensory loss to the mid-calf and wrists, difficulty with fine motor tasks and ambulation on uneven ground — prompting Eloxatin discontinuation at a cumulative dose of approximately 760 mg/m2. Electromyography/nerve conduction studies confirmed a length-dependent, predominantly axonal sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy consistent with oxali injury. Gabapentin 300 mg TID was initiated with modest benefit. Cycles 11 and 12 were completed without oxali (5-FU/LV/bev only). He was also managed for bev-related hypertension throughout induction, requiring addition of amlodipine and ultimately lisinopril.

Following completion of 12 induction cycles, he transitioned to maintenance therapy with 5-FU/leucovorin/Avastin (Avastin 7.5 mg/kg) per OPTIMOX1/CAIRO3-informed strategy — preserving bev backbone to maintain disease control while allowing neuropathy recovery. During the first 2 months of maintenance (approximately months 6-8 from diagnosis), he had stable disease, CEA 79 ng/mL. However, at his most recent restaging CT CAP (approximately 4 months into maintenance, 10 months from diagnosis), there was clear disease progression: two new pulmonary nodules — right lower lobe 1.1 cm and right upper lobe 0.7 cm — new from any prior imaging. There was also slight increase in his segment VI hepatic lesion (2.5 cm, previously 2.3 cm) and a minor increase in the segment VII lesion. CEA rose to 142 ng/mL (from 79). CA 19-9 climbed to 108 U/mL (from 64 at end of induction).

A CT-guided core needle biopsy of the right lower lobe pulmonary nodule was performed by interventional radiology shortly after the progression scan. Pathology returned this week: metastatic moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma, morphology consistent with colorectal primary. IHC: CK20 positive, CDX2 positive, CK7 negative, TTF-1 negative, SATB2 positive. KRAS G12D confirmed by targeted PCR on biopsy tissue — concordant with the primary tumor profile. This confirms that the new pulmonary lesions represent hematogenous spread from the colorectal primary rather than a primary lung malignancy.

Mr. Delgado comes today with his wife for multidisciplinary discussion. He reports no new acute symptoms — some mild fatigue (improved compared to induction), mild persistent tingling in his feet and hands (neuropathy improving), no cough, no hemoptysis, no dyspnea at rest or with mild exertion. Appetite is acceptable; he has regained approximately 3 lbs since completing induction. He returned to part-time teaching approximately 1 month ago. He denies abdominal pain, change in bowel habits, hematochezia, or melena. No chest pain.

MULTIDISCIPLINARY TUMOR BOARD DISCUSSION SUMMARY:

This case was presented at the weekly GI oncology multidisciplinary tumor board with representation from medical oncology, hepatobiliary surgery, colorectal surgery, thoracic surgery, interventional radiology, and diagnostic radiology.

Surgical Perspective — Hepatobiliary Surgery: The hepatic metastases have responded well to FOLFOX/Avastin induction with a nadir at the end of 12 cycles. However, the bilobar distribution persists, and now with emerging pulmonary metastases, the tumor board is not recommending surgical resection at this time. Future liver-directed therapy (hepatic resection, ablation, or TACE) may be revisited if pulmonary disease can be controlled, but currently the dominant concern is systemic disease control.

Thoracic Surgery: The two pulmonary nodules (RLL 1.1 cm, RUL 0.7 cm) are biopsy-confirmed colorectal metastases. At this stage, surgical metachronous pulmonary metastasectomy is deferred given multisite metastatic disease (hepatic + pulmonary) and current disease trajectory. Thoracic surgery would re-evaluate in the context of future disease response.

Medical Oncology Assessment — Treatment Planning: Mr. Delgado has progressed through Eloxatin-based first-line therapy (FOLFOX/Avastin) and through maintenance 5-FU/bev. We must now consider second-line systemic options. The following framework was applied:

1. Anti-EGFR therapy (Erbitux, pani): Absolutely contraindicated due to KRAS G12D mutation. Not a consideration.

2. Continued 5-FU/bev intensification: Unlikely to overcome progression; bev was continued through maintenance and disease broke through. However, the concept of continuing VEGF inhibition beyond progression (per TML/ML18147 data) remains valid — Avastin or an alternative anti-angiogenic agent can be continued with a new chemotherapy backbone.

3. FOLFIRI plus bev: Per ML18147 (TML) trial data, continuing bev beyond first progression with a change to FOLFIRI backbone is a valid strategy with modest but real OS benefit. This would maintain the anti-VEGF mechanism that the patient has been on since diagnosis.

4. FOLFIRI plus ramu: Per the RAISE trial, Camptosar/5-FU/leucovorin (FOLFIRI) plus Cyramza (Cyramza, anti-VEGFR-2 monoclonal antibody) significantly improved both PFS and OS in previously oxali-treated mCRC patients. The advantage of ramu here is a mechanistically distinct anti-angiogenic target (VEGFR-2 versus VEGF-A). This represents a change in anti-angiogenic approach.

5. KRAS G12D targeted therapies: MRTX1133 and other KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors are in active clinical development but are not yet FDA-approved for colorectal cancer. Clinical trial options were discussed with the patient. He understands that KRAS G12D inhibitors are an active area of research and was offered trial referral to our thoracic/GI oncology clinical trials program. Patient has declined trial participation at this time, citing desire to remain close to home, work commitments, and preference for an established regimen with a known safety profile.

Decision: After thorough tumor board discussion and a detailed shared decision-making conversation with Mr. Delgado and his wife, the group elected to proceed with FOLFIRI plus Cyramza as second-line therapy. Rationale: (a) change in cytotoxic backbone from Eloxatin to Camptosar; (b) mechanistically distinct anti-angiogenic agent (VEGFR-2 targeting vs. prior VEGF-A targeting); (c) RAISE trial provides level 1 evidence for this combination in oxali-pretreated mCRC; (d) UGT1A1 *1/*28 status noted — patient is a heterozygous intermediate metabolizer, and standard iri starting dose (180 mg/m2) is appropriate with careful toxicity monitoring, particularly for delayed diarrhea and neutropenia.

Performance status and fitness: ECOG PS 1. He remains fit for combination chemotherapy. Labs support adequate hepatic, renal, and bone marrow reserve to proceed.

Palliative care: Outpatient palliative care was offered and accepted. This will focus on symptom management (neuropathy, fatigue, potential Camptosar-related toxicities), goals of care conversations, and psychosocial support for patient and family.

MICROSCOPIC DESCRIPTION
------------------------------------------------------------------------
Microscopic examination of all submitted sections reveals colonic mucosal fragments extensively replaced and infiltrated by a moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. The tumor demonstrates predominantly tubular glandular architecture with variably sized, irregularly shaped glands lined by stratified columnar epithelium exhibiting moderate nuclear pleomorphism. Nuclei are enlarged, hyperchromatic, and variably vesicular with prominent nucleoli. Mitotic figures are readily identified, including occasional atypical forms. There is a focal area comprising less than 10% of the sampled tumor showing a mucinous/extracellular mucin component with pools of mucin partially distending glands, but the mucinous component does not constitute ≥50% of the lesion and therefore does not qualify as a mucinous (colloid) adenocarcinoma by World Health Organization criteria.

The tumor glands are embedded within a desmoplastic stroma with associated chronic inflammatory infiltrate. Focal areas of necrotic debris ("dirty necrosis") are present within gland lumina, a characteristic feature of colorectal-type adenocarcinoma. No signet ring cells are identified. No poorly differentiated clusters or single infiltrating cells are noted to dominate the picture.

Fragments of non-neoplastic colonic mucosa at the periphery of the biopsy show no significant dysplasia or inflammatory bowel disease pattern. No granulomas identified. No viral cytopathic changes.

Grade: Moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma (Grade 2, ISUP/WHO 2019).

Note: Lymphovascular invasion and perineural invasion cannot be definitively assessed on this superficial biopsy material; clinical staging and resection specimen evaluation will be required for complete staging.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Marcus Delgado is a 59-year-old former smoker and high school teacher with a well-established diagnosis of stage IV metastatic colorectal cancer, now approximately 14 months from initial diagnosis. He was originally diagnosed with a 4.5 cm sigmoid colon primary with synchronous bilobar liver metastases (four lesions, largest 3.8 cm) and was found to harbor a KRAS G12D driver mutation, precluding anti-EGFR therapy. His comprehensive NGS also identified co-mutations in APC, TP53, SMAD4, and PIK3CA, along with MYC amplification, and confirmed MSS/low-TMB disease.

His first-line treatment consisted of FOLFOX plus bevacizumab for 12 planned cycles. He achieved a partial response by imaging after 4 cycles (CEA trending from 487 down to 198 ng/mL; target lesion sum reduced by approximately 25%). Unfortunately, he developed progressive peripheral neuropathy attributed to oxaliplatin — progressing through grade 1 (cycle 3) to grade 2 (cycle 8) and ultimately grade 3 (cycle 10, with bilateral sensorimotor involvement to mid-calf and wrists), necessitating early discontinuation of oxaliplatin after a cumulative dose of approximately 760 mg/m2. He completed his induction course as modified FOLFOX (5-FU/LV/bevacizumab) and transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab. EMG/nerve conduction studies confirmed axonal, length-dependent oxaliplatin-induced neuropathy.

While on maintenance therapy, restaging imaging approximately 10 months from diagnosis identified disease progression in the form of two new pulmonary nodules (right lower lobe 1.1 cm, right upper lobe 0.7 cm), subsequently confirmed as metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma by CT-guided core needle biopsy. Hepatic lesions showed minimal interval increase. CEA had rebounded to 142 ng/mL. This triggered transition to second-line therapy.

After multidisciplinary tumor board review, the decision was made to initiate FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (per the RAISE trial regimen), given prior oxaliplatin exposure/toxicity and bevacizumab use in the first-line setting. His UGT1A1 genotype was *1/*28 (heterozygous), indicating an intermediate metabolizer status, and he was started at standard irinotecan dosing with close toxicity monitoring.

He is now approximately 4 months into second-line therapy (roughly 8 cycles of FOLFIRI/ramucirumab, though irinotecan was dose-reduced to 150 mg/m2 from cycle 4 onward due to grade 2 delayed diarrhea after cycle 3). He also experienced grade 3 hypertension attributed to ramucirumab at cycle 5, which required a one-cycle hold of ramucirumab and intensification of his antihypertensive regimen; ramucirumab was subsequently resumed at full dose after blood pressure was better controlled.

A restaging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed approximately 1-2 weeks ago (after 4 cycles of the FOLFIRI/ramucirumab combination) and the results were reviewed with the patient today. The scan showed disease stabilization: the right lower lobe lung lesion was unchanged at 1.1 cm and the right upper lobe lesion unchanged at 0.7 cm; no new pulmonary nodules. Liver metastases showed a slight decrease in the segment VI lesion (2.3 cm from 2.5 cm) with the remaining three hepatic lesions stable. The sigmoid primary was essentially unchanged at 3.1 cm. The overall sum of target lesions was 5.7 cm compared with 6.1 cm at the time of second-line initiation — representing approximately 7% decrease, consistent with stable disease. CEA was 112 ng/mL, slightly improved from 142 ng/mL at the time of progression.

The patient reports his overall functional status has been relatively preserved. He is fatigued on a day-to-day basis but able to carry out most activities of daily living with some modifications. He has returned to part-time teaching duties, which he finds meaningful and mentally engaging. He continues to experience residual peripheral neuropathy in his bilateral feet, now grade 1 (intermittent tingling, not interfering with ambulation), significantly improved from the grade 3 neuropathy at its nadir. Gabapentin has been gradually tapered and the patient is currently on a reduced dose.

Diarrhea has been manageable with dose reduction and loperamide use as directed. He denies new abdominal pain, rectal bleeding, or significant change in bowel habits beyond his irinotecan-related loose stools. His appetite is reasonably maintained and he has not had significant unintentional weight loss since the last visit.

Today's visit includes a detailed goals-of-care discussion. The patient expressed that he was aware his cancer was incurable and that the goal of treatment was to control the disease and maintain quality of life as long as possible. He expressed a strong desire to continue active anticancer therapy if his body can tolerate it, and emphasized the importance of staying present and functional for his family — his wife and two adult children. He was receptive to palliative care involvement and agreed to proceed with an outpatient palliative care consultation today, as well as a formal palliative care referral for ongoing symptom management co-management going forward.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Marcus Delgado is a 59-year-old man with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D, MSS, TMB-low, left-sided primary) who presents today with unequivocal radiographic and biochemical disease progression after approximately 7 months on second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab. His CEA has more than doubled in 8 weeks and his imaging now shows new pulmonary nodules in both lungs as well as interval growth of the dominant liver metastasis and a new hepatic segment V deposit. This is his second progression event and his disease has now spread to involve the liver, lungs, and sigmoid colon primary (still in situ, not obstructing).

His performance status has declined to ECOG PS 2 — he is managing self-care but his activity level is meaningfully reduced from his baseline. Mild anemia (Hgb 11.1), mildly elevated transaminases and alkaline phosphatase (suggesting modest hepatic parenchymal compromise from increasing metastatic burden), and weight loss of approximately 4 lbs are also noted.

Key oncologic considerations:

1. DISEASE PROGRESSION THROUGH TWO LINES OF THERAPY: He has now progressed through FOLFOX/bevacizumab (first-line) and FOLFIRI/ramucirumab (second-line). KRAS G12D continues to preclude any use of EGFR-directed antibody therapy (cetuximab, panitumumab) regardless of line. His MSS/TMB-low status precludes checkpoint immunotherapy. HER2 is not amplified (IHC 1+, copy number 2.1 on NGS), so HER2-directed therapy is not applicable.

2. THIRD-LINE OPTIONS REVIEWED IN DETAIL:

   a. Regorafenib (Stivarga): A multikinase inhibitor with demonstrated OS benefit in chemotherapy-refractory mCRC (CORRECT trial — median OS 6.4 vs 5.0 months). However, regorafenib carries a significant toxicity burden including hand-foot skin reaction, fatigue, hypertension, and hepatotoxicity. Given Mr. Delgado already has mildly elevated liver function tests and poorly controlled hypertension at baseline, I have concerns about tolerability.

   b. TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) monotherapy: An oral nucleoside analog with demonstrated OS benefit in refractory mCRC (RECOURSE trial). Manageable toxicity profile with primarily myelosuppression. Can be used as a single agent.

   c. TAS-102 plus bevacizumab (SUNLIGHT regimen): Based on the SUNLIGHT trial (published in NEJM 2023), the combination of TAS-102 and bevacizumab demonstrated significantly improved OS compared to TAS-102 monotherapy in refractory mCRC (median OS 10.8 vs 7.5 months; HR 0.61). This combination has emerged as a preferred approach in patients who are fit for this therapy. Mr. Delgado has previously received bevacizumab in first-line without significant toxicity (the dose-limiting toxicity was hypertension driven primarily by the VEGFR-2 mechanism with ramucirumab; bevacizumab-related hypertension was grade 1 in first-line and was managed with single-agent amlodipine).

   d. Clinical trial enrollment: Discussed the landscape of KRAS G12D-directed clinical trials, including investigational KRAS G12D inhibitors (e.g., MRTX1133 in combination studies) and other investigational approaches. Mr. Delgado was counseled on available trials and the potential benefit of enrolling. He has previously declined trial participation, and after today's discussion, he expressed willingness to consider trials but requested to first try an approved regimen. We will submit a referral to our clinical trials office for screening, particularly for any available KRAS G12D-directed studies.

3. PLAN — THIRD-LINE THERAPY: After thorough discussion with Mr. Delgado and his wife, weighing the efficacy data, his current performance status (ECOG 2), his prior treatment exposures, and his goals of care, we will initiate third-line TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) 35 mg/m2 orally twice daily with food on days 1-5 and days 8-12 of each 28-day cycle, in combination with bevacizumab 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (days 1 and 15), per the SUNLIGHT trial regimen. I reviewed the oral medication dosing schedule carefully with him and his wife to ensure understanding — he will take the tablets twice daily (morning and evening) with meals on the designated days, and return every 2 weeks for bevacizumab infusions and CBC checks.

   Dose calculation: BSA approximately 1.95 m². TAS-102 dose: 35 mg/m² = ~68 mg per dose, rounded to 70 mg per dose (using available 15 mg and 20 mg tablet combinations). Bevacizumab: 5 mg/kg = ~395 mg (rounded per pharmacy).

   Monitoring: CBC with differential every 2 weeks, given myelosuppression as the primary TAS-102 toxicity. CMP and LFTs every cycle. Blood pressure monitoring at each infusion. Urinalysis for proteinuria each cycle (bevacizumab requirement). Tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) and CT restaging after 3 cycles (approximately 3 months).

4. PERFORMANCE STATUS AND SUPPORTIVE CARE: His ECOG PS has declined to 2. This is an important consideration as further PS decline may limit tolerability of even this relatively less toxic regimen. Reinforcing nutritional support — he has mild hypoalbuminemia and unintentional weight loss. Referred to clinical dietitian (appointment to be scheduled). Continued optimization of anemia is warranted: iron studies and B12/folate levels ordered today. If iron-deficient anemia identified, will treat with iron supplementation. ESA use is not indicated in this actively treated metastatic setting.

5. PAIN AND SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT: Right upper quadrant discomfort is currently mild and managed adequately with acetaminophen. If pain worsens, will reassess and escalate analgesic therapy (low-dose opioid may be appropriate if acetaminophen insufficient). Nausea prophylaxis: ondansetron PRN. Fatigue: I advised reasonable pacing of activity, brief naps if needed, and avoidance of isolation — maintaining social engagement with family has been shown to support quality of life. No formal prescription for a CNS stimulant at this time given his performance status and the fact that he is starting a new regimen.

6. PALLIATIVE CARE: Mr. Delgado has been engaged with outpatient palliative care since approximately month 14 of his illness, and I strongly encourage continued close co-management. As his disease has now progressed through two lines of therapy with a declining performance status, the palliative care team's involvement in goals-of-care conversations, advance care planning, and symptom management will become increasingly important. I have communicated today's visit summary to the palliative care team.

7. GOALS OF CARE: We had a frank and compassionate discussion today about the trajectory of his illness. He understands that his disease has progressed through two systemic therapy regimens and that each line of therapy, statistically, tends to yield smaller responses and shorter durations of disease control. He was told clearly that TAS-102/bevacizumab represents a reasonable option with data supporting a median survival of approximately 10-11 months in the trial population, but that individual outcomes vary widely. He expressed that he values being able to remain active and present for his wife and children, to continue some teaching if possible, and to avoid hospitalizations. He does not wish to discuss hospice at this time but has acknowledged that this would be an appropriate future discussion as circumstances evolve. His values and wishes were documented.

8. HYPERTENSION: Blood pressure today 148/92 mmHg — mildly elevated on current triple regimen. He had an episode of grade 3 hypertension on ramucirumab (VEGFR-2-directed mechanism); bevacizumab in first-line was associated with grade 1 HTN. I anticipate bevacizumab will be better tolerated from a blood pressure standpoint. Will obtain repeat BP reading at end of visit. If persistently above 145/90 mmHg, will contact cardiology and consider further antihypertensive adjustment.

9. DIABETES MANAGEMENT: Metformin continued. HbA1c 7.1% — acceptable. Will monitor given that corticosteroid use as pre-medication (dexamethasone) may cause transient hyperglycemia; patient and care team should be aware.

Pharmacy will process the TAS-102 (Lonsurf) prescription for oral dispensing today. PLAN:

ONCOLOGIC MANAGEMENT:
This is a pivotal and difficult visit. Mr. Delgado has received three lines of systemic therapy over the course of approximately 21 months. His current regimen (TAS-102/bevacizumab) is showing marginal, likely tenuous, disease control — imaging is borderline stable but markers are rising, functional status has declined meaningfully, and new ascites signals peritoneal involvement. We have had an extended, candid, and compassionate discussion today with Mr. Delgado, his wife Claudia, and his adult daughter regarding the trajectory of his illness.

We discussed:
- The current disease status in accessible, non-technical language: "The scan from two weeks ago showed the cancer is not dramatically worse, but it's also not responding — it's essentially holding its ground at best, while you are feeling significantly worse. The blood marker has continued to climb, and we've seen a small amount of new fluid in the abdomen, which tells us the cancer has spread to the lining of the belly — a new site we haven't seen before."
- The expected prognosis honestly and gently: this is an advanced, treatment-refractory malignancy. Given his trajectory through three lines of therapy, declining PS, rising markers, peritoneal extension, and worsening hepatic function, the median prognosis at this juncture is measured in months.
- Fourth-line therapy options: regorafenib (which he did not previously receive) and combination therapies. However, in the context of ECOG PS 2-3, declining hepatic reserve, and worsening functional status, the likelihood of meaningful clinical benefit from additional cytotoxic therapy is low, and the risk of treatment-related harm is high. This was discussed openly.
- Clinical trial options: KRAS G12D-targeted therapies (e.g., MRTX1133 and related agents in development) remain investigational and not available outside of clinical trials. We discussed that while he had previously declined trial enrollment, the option remains open if he wishes to pursue this, and we will facilitate referral to a major academic center if desired. However, enrollment eligibility will depend on PS criteria and his ability to travel and tolerate protocol requirements.

After extensive conversation, Mr. Delgado stated clearly and repeatedly, with full understanding of the implications: "I want to keep fighting, but I don't want to feel worse than I already do. I want to be home with my family. If the treatment is going to take away the time I have left, I don't want it." His wife and daughter affirmed his wishes.

Decision regarding TAS-102/bevacizumab: We will continue the current regimen for now at the dose-reduced TAS-102 level (30 mg/m2), given that the imaging showed marginal stability. We acknowledge this is a time-limited trial of continued therapy. If the patient's functional status worsens further, or if the next imaging demonstrates clear progression, we will discontinue all cytotoxic therapy and transition fully to comfort-focused hospice care. The patient and family agree with and endorse this plan explicitly.

Bevacizumab — given the new ascites and peritoneal involvement, we will continue bevacizumab as part of the current regimen for its anti-VEGF effect on both the metastatic disease and potentially the ascites. We will reassess at next visit.

Next CT restaging: planned in approximately 6-8 weeks. If disease has clearly worsened (particularly hepatic or peritoneal burden), we will have an immediate goals-of-care reassessment.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Delgado was initially diagnosed approximately 23 months ago with stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar liver metastases. His tumor harbors a KRAS G12D driver mutation (VAF 42%), co-occurring with APC, TP53, SMAD4, PIK3CA, and FBXW7 mutations, consistent with a genomically complex right-shifted colorectal cancer biology. His disease has been microsatellite stable (MMR-proficient) with low tumor mutational burden (3.2 mut/Mb), rendering him ineligible for checkpoint immunotherapy.

He received first-line FOLFOX (oxali 85 mg/m2, later dose-reduced to 75 mg/m2) plus bev for 12 cycles, achieving a meaningful partial response — his liver metastases shrank substantially (segment VI from 3.8 cm to 2.3 cm; sum of targets down 43%) and his CEA fell from 487 to 87 ng/mL. Unfortunately, the cumulative oxali dose drove progressive peripheral neuropathy, ultimately reaching grade 3 with significant functional impairment (numbness to mid-calf and wrists, difficulty walking on uneven surfaces, objects dropped), necessitating Eloxatin discontinuation at cycle 10. He then transitioned to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/Avastin per an OPTIMOX-informed strategy.

At approximately 10 months from diagnosis, restaging imaging on maintenance therapy revealed new pulmonary metastases (right lower lobe 1.1 cm, right upper lobe 0.7 cm) alongside slight hepatic progression — documented as his first progression event. A CT-guided lung biopsy confirmed metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with concordant KRAS G12D status. He was transitioned to second-line FOLFIRI plus Cyramza (RAISE regimen), which achieved disease stabilization for approximately 7 months. His course was complicated by Camptosar-related grade 2 delayed diarrhea (managed with loperamide and dose reduction to 150 mg/m2) and Cyramza-related grade 3 hypertension (requiring triple antihypertensive therapy and one cycle hold). He ultimately progressed at approximately 17 months from diagnosis with new pulmonary nodules (left lower lobe, right middle lobe, lingula), new hepatic lesion in segment V, and a rising CEA to 287 ng/mL.

In the ensuing 2 months, Mr. Delgado has experienced significant clinical deterioration: weight loss of approximately 8 lbs over 2 months, worsening fatigue, diminished appetite, early satiety, and increasing abdominal discomfort.
